title: Chapter 8 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of useEffect and side effects!

questions.question: What is a side effect in React?
options: Any operation that affects something outside the component's render
questions.explanation: Side effects are interactions with the outside world, like fetching data, timers, or manual DOM changes.

questions.question: useEffect always runs after the browser paints the screen.
questions.explanation: Yes, effects run after layout and paint, ensuring they don't block visual updates.

questions.question: Which dependency array causes useEffect to run only once after the initial render?
questions.explanation: An empty array `[]` means no dependencies, so the effect runs only on mount.

questions.question: When does the cleanup function in useEffect run?
options: Before the effect runs again and when the component unmounts
questions.explanation: Cleanup runs before the effect re-runs (if dependencies change) and on unmount.

questions.question: You can make the useEffect callback async directly.
questions.explanation: No, useEffect expects a synchronous function or a cleanup function. You define an async function inside.

questions.question: What problem does the 'ignore' flag solve in data fetching with useEffect?
options: Prevents setting state on an unmounted component
questions.explanation: It avoids memory leaks by ignoring the fetch result if the component unmounted.

questions.question: If you omit a variable from the dependency array that is used inside useEffect, you may have a _____ closure.
questions.explanation: Stale closure means the effect sees an old value of the variable.
Updating `count` inside the effect triggers a re-render, and because `count` is a dependency, the effect runs again, causing an infinite loop.

questions.question: It's recommended to have multiple useEffect calls for unrelated logic.
questions.explanation: Yes, separating concerns makes the code easier to understand and maintain.
With no dependency array, the effect runs after every render, so it runs 5 times (once after each render).

questions.question: The function returned from useEffect is called the _____ function.
questions.explanation: It's commonly called the cleanup function.

questions.question: What is the correct way to set up an interval that updates state every second?
options: useEffect(() => { const id = setInterval(() => setCount(c => c + 1), 1000); return () => clearInterval(id); }, []);
questions.explanation: Option 2 uses a functional update to avoid stale closure and includes cleanup. Option 1 and 3 have stale closure issues. Option 4 creates an infinite loop.

questions.question: If you pass an object literal as a dependency, the effect will run on every render.
questions.explanation: Yes, because object literals are recreated on each render, so they are always different.

options: Memory leak by removing the listener on unmount
questions.explanation: It prevents memory leaks and ensures the listener is removed, avoiding duplicate listeners if the effect ran again (but with [] it won't).

questions.question: When does React run the cleanup function for an effect with dependencies [a, b]?
options: Before the effect runs again if a or b changed
questions.explanation: Cleanup runs before the effect re-runs on dependency change and also on unmount.

questions.question: You can use async/await directly inside useEffect by marking the callback as async.
questions.explanation: No, because an async function returns a promise, but useEffect expects either nothing or a cleanup function. You must define an async function inside and call it.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a side effect?
questions.explanation: Calculating a value from props is pure and part of rendering, not a side effect.

questions.question: To run an effect only when a specific value changes, you put that value in the _____ array.
questions.explanation: The dependency array controls when the effect re-runs.

questions.question: Effects run in the order they are defined, and cleanup runs in reverse order.
questions.explanation: Yes, React guarantees this order.

questions.question: What is a common solution to avoid stale closures in setInterval?
options: Use a ref to store the latest state
Use the functional update form of setState
Move the interval outside the component
questions.explanation: Functional updates (setCount(c => c+1)) avoid stale state, or you can use a ref to keep the latest callback.
